function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing retrospectively recasts historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers. What historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment?

steps.reasoning_content:
The question asks about what historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment, given that the SpaceX S-1 filing retrospectively recasts historical financials under common-control accounting following the mergers (xAI merged February 2, 2026, and X Holdings merged into xAI March 28, 2025).

Let me first parse the filing to understand the common-control accounting treatment and what it means for historical financial observability.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Now let me retrieve information about the common-control accounting treatment and what it means for historical financials, particularly compared to purchase accounting.

arguments.prompt: What is the common-control accounting treatment and how does it affect historical financials in {{spacex_s1}}? What is the difference between common-control accounting and purchase accounting?
What does the filing say about retrospective recast, basis of presentation, and mergers with xAI and X Holdings in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is the breakdown of the common-control accounting treatment, its effect on historical financials, and how it differs from standard purchase accounting.

### 1. Common-Control Accounting Treatment & Effect on Historical Financials
Because Elon Musk held a controlling financial interest (majority voting interest) in SpaceX, xAI, and X during all presented periods, the mergers were not treated as standard arm's-length acquisitions. Instead, they were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**, which constitutes a "change in reporting entity."

**How it affects the historical financials:**
*   **Retrospective Combination:** The consolidated financial statements are recast to combine the net assets and historical results of SpaceX, xAI, and X for *all periods presented*, as if they had always been a single combined entity.
*   **Historical Carrying Amounts:** The net assets of the acquired entities are combined at their historical book values (carrying amounts) rather than being revalued.
*   **No Goodwill or Intangibles:** The text explicitly notes that *no new goodwill or other intangible assets* have been recorded as a result of the mergers.
*   **Elimination of Related-Party Transactions:** All historical transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X that were previously considered "related-party" are retroactively eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Retroactive Share Count Adjustments:** Historical common stock share counts for X and xAI are retroactively adjusted using the merger exchange ratios (and the 2026 five-for-one stock split) so that all historical common shares are presented as SpaceX Common Stock equivalents.
*   **Omission of Separate Financials:** Because the historical financials are fully recast to include xAI and X, the company is not required to provide separate, standalone financial statements for those acquired entities.
*   *Nuance regarding Preferred Stock:* While common stock is recast retrospectively, **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** is an exception. Because it was a legally distinct equity class prior to the merger, it is presented in its historical legal form for prior periods and is only converted into SpaceX Common Stock *prospectively* in Q1 2026 (the period the merger actually occurred).

---

### 2. Common-Control Accounting vs. Purchase Accounting
While the provided text does not explicitly use the term "purchase accounting" (also known as the acquisition method), it highlights the exact mechanics of common-control accounting by explicitly stating what it *does not* do. 

Here is how common-control accounting differs from a standard third-party purchase acquisition:

| Feature | Common-Control Accounting (SpaceX/xAI/X Mergers) | Standard Purchase Accounting (Arm's-Length Acquisitions) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Timeline of Consolidation** | **Retrospective:** Financials are recast for *all periods presented* as if the entities were always combined. | **Prospective:** The acquirer only includes the target's financial results from the exact date of acquisition forward. Prior historical periods are *not* restated. |
| **Asset & Liability Valuation** | **Historical Carrying Amounts:** Assets and liabilities are transferred at their existing historical book values. | **Fair Value:** Assets and liabilities are "stepped up" or "stepped down" to their fair market value on the acquisition date. |
| **Goodwill & Intangibles** | **None:** The text explicitly states *no new goodwill or other intangible assets* are recorded. | **Recognized:** Goodwill is typically created if the purchase price exceeds the fair value of the net identifiable assets. New intangible assets are also recognized. |
| **Nature of Transaction** | Viewed as an internal **reorganization** or restructuring of entities already under the same ultimate boss. | Viewed as an **acquisition** of a new, unrelated business from a third party. |
| **Equity Treatment** | Historical equity of both entities is combined and adjusted by exchange ratios to reflect a continuation of the historical entity. | The target company's historical equity is eliminated; the acquirer issues new shares or pays cash, and only the acquirer's historical equity remains. |

Based on the provided prospectus excerpts, here is a detailed breakdown of what the filing says regarding the mergers with xAI and X Holdings, the retrospective recasting of financials, and the overall basis of presentation.

### 1. Mergers with xAI and X Holdings
The filing details a two-step consolidation of Elon Musk’s AI and social media ventures into SpaceX, both of which were executed as share exchanges and accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control** (since Musk held majority voting interest in all entities involved).

*   **The X Merger (March 28, 2025):** xAI acquired X Holdings Corp. (“X”) and X.AI Corp., making them wholly-owned subsidiaries of xAI. 
    *   *Conversion Ratios:* X Common Stock converted into 2.776 shares of xAI Common Stock. X.AI Corp. Common and Preferred Stock converted on a 1:1 basis into xAI Common and xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock.
*   **The xAI Merger (February 2, 2026):** SpaceX acquired X.AI Holdings Corp. (“xAI”), making it a wholly-owned subsidiary of SpaceX.
    *   *Consideration:* SpaceX issued 321.7 million Class A shares, 121.7 million Class B shares (on a pre-split basis), and paid $2.947 billion in cash to xAI stockholders.
    *   *Conversion Ratios:* xAI Common Stock converted into SpaceX Common Stock at a fixed exchange ratio of **0.1433** SpaceX shares per xAI share. Eligible service providers were given a cash election option of $75.46 per share.
    *   *Warrants:* Outstanding xAI customer warrants triggered an acceleration clause, automatically exercising on a cashless basis into SpaceX Class A Common Stock at the 0.1433 ratio.
*   **Tesla’s Investment:** In January 2026, Tesla agreed to invest $2 billion in xAI Series E Preferred Stock. Following the xAI Merger and regulatory approvals, this right converted into SpaceX Class A Common Stock, resulting in SpaceX issuing 3.8 million shares to Tesla in March 2026.
*   **2026 Stock Split:** On May 4, 2026, SpaceX executed a 5-for-1 forward stock split of its Class A, B, and C Common Stock. 

### 2. Retrospective Recast
Because the mergers were between entities under common control, SpaceX was required to retrospectively recast its financial statements to present the companies as if they had always been combined. 

*   **Historical Combination:** The consolidated financial statements reflect the retrospective combination of the net assets of SpaceX, xAI, and X at their **historical carrying amounts** for all periods presented. 
*   **Eliminations & Goodwill:** No new goodwill or intangible assets were recorded as a result of the mergers. All historical related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X have been eliminated in consolidation.
*   **Common Stock Recast:** Historical X, X.AI Corp., and xAI Common Stock are presented in the historical financial statements as if they had been converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the applicable exchange rates for all periods presented.
*   **Exception for xAI Preferred Stock:** xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock is **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted basis prior to the merger date. Because it was a legally distinct equity class of a predecessor entity, historical balance sheets continue to reflect it as outstanding xAI Preferred Stock. The conversion into SpaceX Common Stock is only recognized prospectively in the period the merger actually occurred (Q1 2026).
*   **Stock Split Adjustment:** All historical share and per-share information has been retroactively adjusted to reflect the May 2026 5-for-1 stock split.
*   **Change in Reporting Entity:** This retrospective combination constitutes a "change in reporting entity." Consequently, separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided.

### 3. Basis of Presentation
The financial statements and accompanying notes establish the foundational accounting rules and operational scope of the newly combined entity.

*   **U.S. GAAP & Consolidation:** The financials are prepared in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP). They include the accounts of SpaceX and its wholly-owned subsidiaries, with all intercompany balances and transactions eliminated.
*   **Use of Estimates:** Preparation requires management to make significant estimates and assumptions that affect reported assets, liabilities, revenues, and expenses. Key areas of judgment include cost-to-cost revenue recognition, deferred tax valuation allowances, inventory reserves, and the fair value/useful lives of goodwill and indefinite-lived intangibles.
*   **Operational Scope (Segments):** The basis of presentation now encompasses SpaceX's core aerospace and Starlink connectivity operations, alongside a new **AI segment**. This AI segment operates a vertically integrated platform spanning the Grok frontier LLM, enterprise/consumer AI solutions, computational infrastructure, and the X platform (described as a "real-time information, entertainment, and free speech platform").
*   **Limitations of Historical Data:** The filing explicitly cautions that the summary historical consolidated financial and operating data (which now includes the combined footprint of SpaceX, xAI, and X) is not necessarily indicative of future results or full fiscal periods.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now let me retrieve more specific information about what is no longer observable under common-control accounting versus purchase accounting. The key question is: what historical information is "no longer observable" relative to purchase accounting treatment?

Under purchase accounting (ASC 805), you would typically see:
1. Fair value step-ups of acquired assets and liabilities
2. Goodwill recognition
3. New intangible assets recognized
4. Acquisition-date fair value disclosures
5. Pro forma financial information showing what the combined entity would have looked like
6. Separate pre-acquisition financials of the acquired entity
7. The purchase price allocation

Under common-control accounting, none of these are present because:
- Assets/liabilities are carried at historical book value (no fair value step-up)
- No goodwill is created
- No new intangible assets are recognized
- Historical financials are fully recast (so you can't see standalone SpaceX or standalone xAI/X results)
- Related-party transactions are eliminated retroactively

Let me search for more specific language about what is no longer observable.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about goodwill, intangible assets, fair value step-up, purchase price allocation, and what is not recorded under common-control accounting in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about standalone financials, separate financial statements, related-party transactions eliminated, and limitations of historical data in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the prospectus and financial statement notes, here is the breakdown of how the company handles goodwill, intangible assets, fair value step-ups, purchase price allocations, and the specific accounting treatments for its major acquisitions.

### 1. Common-Control Accounting & What is NOT Recorded
The acquisitions of **xAI** (by SpaceX in Feb 2026) and **X / Twitter** (by xAI in March 2025) were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**, because Elon Musk held a controlling financial/majority voting interest in all entities involved. 

Because of this specific accounting treatment, the following are **NOT recorded**:
*   **No Fair Value Step-Up:** The net assets of xAI and X were *not* marked up to their current fair market values. Instead, the consolidated financial statements reflect a retrospective combination of the entities' net assets at their **historical carrying amounts** (historical cost).
*   **No Purchase Price Allocation:** Because the assets and liabilities simply carry over at their historical book values, a traditional purchase price allocation (assigning fair values to acquired assets and liabilities) was not performed for these mergers.
*   **No New Goodwill or Intangibles:** The filing explicitly states: *"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded"* as a result of the xAI and X mergers. 
*   **Eliminations:** All historical related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X were eliminated in consolidation.

### 2. Goodwill
*   **Definition & Policy:** Goodwill represents the excess of the purchase price over the fair value of identifiable assets and assumed liabilities in a standard business combination. It is not amortized but is tested for impairment annually (October 1) or when triggering events occur.
*   **Balances & Allocation:** As of March 31, 2026, total goodwill was **$11,681 million**. It is heavily concentrated in the AI segment:
    *   **AI Segment:** $11,166 million
    *   **Connectivity Segment:** $515 million
*   **Impairment:** The company conducted annual tests and noted that **no goodwill impairments** were identified for the years ended December 31, 2023, 2024, and 2025.

### 3. Intangible Assets
*   **The "X" Rebranding Impairment:** In 2023, the company rebranded Twitter to X. This triggered an impairment assessment, resulting in a massive **$3,775 million impairment charge** on the previously indefinite-lived brand intangible (attributed to the AI segment). Consequently, the brand was reclassified as a **finite-lived intangible asset** with a 5-year useful life, valued using the relief-from-royalty method.
*   **Indefinite-Lived Intangibles:** As of March 31, 2026, the company held $19 million in indefinite-lived intangibles, consisting primarily of trade names and domain names.
*   **Pending Spectrum Acquisition:** In September 2025, the company entered into a Spectrum License Purchase Agreement with EchoStar (total consideration involving billions in cash and debt satisfaction). Because the transaction has not yet closed (expected Nov 2027), it is not yet on the balance sheet, but the filing notes that once it closes, the spectrum licenses will be recognized as **acquired intangible assets**.

### 4. Fair Value Step-Up & Purchase Price Allocation
*   **Absence in Mergers:** As detailed in Section 1, fair value step-ups and purchase price allocations are entirely absent regarding the SpaceX/xAI/X mergers due to the common-control accounting rules (ASC 805-50), which mandate the use of historical carrying amounts rather than fair value.
*   **Application of Fair Value Elsewhere:** While not used for M&A step-ups in this context, the company strictly applies the **ASC 820 Fair Value Hierarchy (Levels I, II, and III)** to other areas of its balance sheet:
    *   **Digital Assets (Bitcoin):** Measured at fair value on a recurring basis using Level I inputs (quoted prices on active exchanges). As of Dec 31, 2025, the company held 18,712 Bitcoin with a cost basis of $661 million and a fair value of $1,637 million.
    *   **Impairment Testing:** Fair value (using discounted cash flows and market approaches) is used to test reporting units and indefinite-lived intangibles for impairment.
    *   **Financial Instruments:** Cash equivalents, marketable securities, and restricted cash are measured at fair value based on observable market data (Levels I and II).

Based on the provided excerpts from the S-1 filing, here is what the document says regarding your specific queries:

**Separate Financial Statements (Standalone Financials)**
*   **Not Provided:** The filing explicitly states that **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**. 
*   **Reasoning:** This is because the consolidated financial statements already reflect the retrospective combination and reorganization of these entities under common control for all periods presented, rendering separate standalone statements unnecessary.

**Related-Party Transactions Eliminated**
*   **Elimination in Consolidation:** Because the acquisitions of xAI and X Holdings were transactions between entities under common control (controlled by Elon Musk), the company retrospectively combined their net assets at historical carrying amounts. As part of this accounting treatment, **all historical related-party transactions between these entities have been eliminated in consolidation.**
*   **No New Goodwill:** The filing notes that no new goodwill or other intangible assets were recorded as a result of this retrospective combination.

**Limitations of Historical Data**
The filing outlines several strict limitations and caveats regarding the "Summary Historical Consolidated Financial and Operating Data":
*   **Not Predictive of Future Results:** The historical data presented is "not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period."
*   **Interim vs. Full Year:** The results for any interim period (such as the three months ended March 31) are "not necessarily indicative of the results to be expected for the full fiscal period."
*   **Not a Replacement for Full Statements:** The summary data is "not intended to replace the consolidated financial statements." It is qualified in its entirety by the full, audited/unaudited consolidated financial statements and related notes included elsewhere in the prospectus.
*   **Subject to Estimates:** The underlying financial statements require management to make significant estimates and assumptions (e.g., regarding over-time revenue recognition, tax liabilities, and inventory reserves), meaning actual future results could differ from these historical estimates.

Let me also check if there's any discussion about the pre-merger standalone financials of SpaceX being unavailable.

arguments.prompt: What does the filing say about standalone SpaceX operations, pre-merger financials, and what investors cannot observe from the historical financials in {{spacex_s1}}?
What does the filing say about the exchange ratio, consideration paid, fair value of consideration, and the $2.947 billion cash payment in the xAI merger in {{spacex_s1}}?

results.content:
Based on the provided excerpts from the S-1 prospectus, here is what the filing reveals regarding standalone SpaceX operations, pre-merger financials, and the limitations of the historical financial statements:

### 1. Standalone SpaceX Operations (Space & Connectivity)
Before the integration of xAI and X, SpaceX’s core operations consisted of two primary segments, which the filing details extensively:
*   **Space Segment:** Focuses on designing, manufacturing, and launching reusable rockets (Falcon and Starship). It achieved sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA starting in **2018**. In 2025, the Space segment generated an operating loss of $(657) million but achieved a Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $653 million (which included the impact of funding $3,004 million in R&D). Operationally, it completed 170 missions in 2025 and launched over 2,200 metric tons to orbit (over 80% of the global mass to orbit for the year).
*   **Connectivity Segment (Starlink):** Operates the low-Earth orbit satellite broadband network. It was funded initially by the Space segment's EBITDA and external equity, and it achieved sustained positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA starting in **2023**. As of March 31, 2026, it had approximately 9,600 satellites and 10.3 million subscribers across 164 markets.
*   **Capital Allocation Strategy:** The filing highlights a "stellar track record" of using cash flows from the foundational Space segment to fund the Connectivity segment, creating a flywheel of predictable, recurring revenue. 

### 2. Pre-Merger Financials & Accounting Treatment
Because the acquisitions of X.AI Holdings (effective Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (effective March 28, 2025) were considered **transactions between entities under common control**, the financials do not look like a standard M&A acquisition:
*   **Retrospective Recasting:** The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast for *all periods presented* to combine the historical results of SpaceX, xAI, and X. 
*   **No New Goodwill:** The assets and liabilities of the merged entities were combined at their historical carrying amounts. No new goodwill or intangible assets were recorded.
*   **Stock Split Adjustment:** A 5-for-1 stock split that occurred on May 4, 2026, has also been retroactively adjusted across all historical share and per-share data presented in the filing.
*   **Elimination of Internal Transactions:** All historical related-party transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X prior to the mergers have been eliminated in consolidation.

### 3. What Investors *Cannot* Observe from the Historical Financials
The filing includes several explicit warnings and accounting caveats about what the historical data obscures or fails to represent:
*   **Future Predictive Value:** The summary historical consolidated financial and operating data is **not indicative of the results to be expected for any future period**, nor are interim results necessarily indicative of full-year results.
*   **An "As-Converted" Historical View of xAI Preferred Stock:** Although xAI’s Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock converted into SpaceX Common Stock upon the merger, the historical balance sheets **are not recast on an as-converted basis**. Because it was a legally distinct class of equity prior to the merger, it is presented in its historical legal form. Investors cannot observe the "as-converted" historical impact; the conversion is only recognized prospectively in Q1 2026.
*   **Standalone (Unmerged) SpaceX Financials:** Because the financials are retrospectively combined under common control accounting, investors cannot look at the primary consolidated financial statements and easily isolate the strict, legally standalone historical financials of SpaceX (excluding xAI and X) prior to the mergers. 
*   **Historical Related-Party Friction:** Because all historical related-party transactions between the commonly controlled entities (SpaceX, xAI, and X) were eliminated in consolidation, investors cannot observe the historical scale, pricing, or financial impact of the internal dealings between these entities before they were formally merged.

Based on the provided excerpts from the S-1 prospectus preamble, here is the breakdown of the exchange ratios, consideration paid, fair value accounting, and the $2.947 billion cash payment regarding the xAI Merger:

### 1. Exchange Ratio
The filing establishes a fixed, pre-2026 Stock Split exchange ratio of **0.1433** for converting xAI equity into SpaceX equity, alongside specific ratios for the X Merger:
*   **xAI Preferred Stock:** Series A-1, B, C, D, and E ("Low Vote") converted into **0.1433** shares of SpaceX Class A Common Stock. Series A ("High Vote") converted into **0.1433** shares of SpaceX Class B Common Stock.
*   **xAI Common Stock:** All classes (Class A, Class B, and Limited Voting) converted into SpaceX Common Stock at the **0.1433** ratio.
*   **xAI Equity Awards & Warrants:** Options, RSUs, RSAs, and customer warrants converted into SpaceX equivalents at the **0.1433** ratio. (For options, the exercise price was divided by 0.1433).
*   **X Holdings Corp. (X Merger):** X Common Stock converted into xAI Common Stock at a ratio of **2.776** (which then subsequently converts to SpaceX at the 0.1433 ratio). X.AI Corp. common and preferred stock converted at a **1.000** ratio into xAI equivalents.
*   *Fractional Shares:* Fractional SpaceX shares were rounded *up* to the nearest whole number for xAI preferred/common stock, but rounded *down* for X Common Stock.

### 2. Consideration Paid
To effectuate the xAI Merger on February 2, 2026, SpaceX paid the following consideration to xAI stockholders:
*   **Stock Issuance:** SpaceX issued **321,681,643 shares of Class A Common Stock** and **121,683,400 shares of Class B Common Stock** as partial consideration. 
    * *Note:* This Class A issuance includes 3,798,039 shares issued to Tesla, which converted Tesla's prior $2 billion investment in xAI Series E Preferred Stock into SpaceX Class A stock following regulatory approval.
*   **Cash Election Option:** "Eligible service providers" holding xAI Series A Preferred Stock or xAI Class A/B Common Stock were granted the right to elect cash instead of stock, priced at **$75.46 per share** (on a pre-2026 Stock Split basis).
*   **Cash Payment:** A massive lump-sum cash payment (detailed in section 4 below).

### 3. Fair Value of Consideration
*   **No Fair Value Recast (Common Control Accounting):** The filing explicitly states that because Elon Musk held a controlling financial/majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X, the mergers were accounted for as **reorganizations of entities under common control**. Therefore, the entities were combined at their **historical carrying amounts**, *not* fair value. No new goodwill or intangible assets were recorded, and historical financial statements were retrospectively combined rather than undergoing a fair-value purchase price allocation.
*   **CEO Award Fair Value:** The only specific "fair value" calculations provided in the text relate to the grant date fair value assumptions for the CEO's xAI performance stock award (using an expected term of 10.0 years, volatility of 45%–55%, risk-free rate of 4.06%, and 0.00% dividend yield).

### 4. The $2.947 Billion Cash Payment
*   **The Payment:** Pursuant to the terms of the xAI Merger on February 2, 2026, alongside the issuance of Class A and Class B stock, SpaceX **paid $2,947 million ($2.947 billion) in cash** to holders of xAI Common Stock and Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock.
*   **Context & Accounting Impact:** This cash outlay was a core component of the merger consideration. It likely corresponds to the cash elections made by eligible service providers (who opted for the $75.46/share payout), the cashless exercise mechanics of certain warrants, or other specific merger agreement provisions requiring cash settlement. 
*   **Balance Sheet Reflection:** In the *Consolidated Statement of Changes in Equity*, this massive cash outflow appears to be heavily reflected in the line item **"Repurchase of common stock pursuant to xAI Merger,"** which shows a **$(2,413) million** reduction to Additional Paid-in Capital and Total Shareholders' Equity, representing the accounting treatment of cash paid out to retiring xAI equity holders.

steps.message: Now I have a thorough understanding of the accounting treatment. Let me compile the comprehensive answer.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the question. Let me synthesize the answer about what historical information is no longer observable relative to purchase accounting treatment.

Under purchase accounting (ASC 805), the following would typically be observable:
1. Fair value step-up of acquired assets and liabilities
2. Goodwill recognition (excess of purchase price over fair value of net identifiable assets)
3. New intangible assets recognized (e.g., customer relationships, technology, brand)
4. Purchase price allocation disclosure
5. Pro forma financial information showing combined entity as if acquisition happened earlier
6. Separate pre-acquisition financial statements of the acquired entity
7. The acquirer's standalone historical financials (pre-acquisition) remain unchanged
8. Related-party transactions between the entities would still be visible pre-acquisition

Under common-control accounting (as applied in this filing), the following is no longer observable:
1. **Fair value of acquired net assets** - assets/liabilities carried at historical book value, not fair value
2. **Goodwill from the mergers** - no new goodwill recorded (though existing goodwill from prior acquisitions like Twitter's original acquisition by X/Musk remains)
3. **New intangible assets** - no new intangibles recognized from the mergers
4. **Purchase price allocation** - no allocation performed
5. **Standalone SpaceX financials** - cannot isolate pre-merger SpaceX-only results because everything is retrospectively combined
6. **Standalone xAI/X financials** - separate financial statements not provided
7. **Historical related-party transactions** - all eliminated retroactively, so the scale, pricing, and nature of inter-company dealings are invisible
8. **The implied valuation of the mergers** - while we know the consideration ($2.947B cash + shares), there's no fair value measurement of the net assets acquired, so the implied premium/discount is not calculable in the traditional sense

Let me now compose a comprehensive answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Historical Information No Longer Observable Under Common-Control Accounting vs. Purchase Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 retrospectively recasts all historical periods under **common-control accounting** (ASC 805-50) because Elon Musk held a controlling/majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings during all periods presented. This treatment fundamentally differs from purchase accounting (ASC 805 acquisition method) and renders the following categories of historical information **no longer observable**:

---

### 1. Fair Value of Acquired Net Assets
Under purchase accounting, the acquirer must remeasure all acquired assets and assumed liabilities to their **acquisition-date fair values** (the "step-up" or "step-down"). Under common-control accounting, the net assets of xAI and X were combined into SpaceX's consolidated financials at their **historical carrying amounts** (book values). Investors cannot observe what the fair value of xAI's or X's net assets—such as AI infrastructure, the Grok model, the X platform, user data, or spectrum rights—would have been at the merger dates.

### 2. Merger-Generated Goodwill
Purchase accounting would have recognized **goodwill** equal to the excess of the purchase consideration over the fair value of identifiable net assets. Given that SpaceX paid $2.947 billion in cash plus 321.7 million Class A shares and 121.7 million Class B shares (pre-split) for xAI, a substantial goodwill balance would likely have been created. The filing explicitly states: **"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded"** as a result of the mergers. The $11,681 million in goodwill on the consolidated balance sheet as of March 31, 2026 (of which $11,166 million is in the AI segment) reflects only **pre-existing goodwill** from X's original 2022 take-private acquisition—not any incremental goodwill from the SpaceX/xAI or xAI/X mergers.

### 3. New Intangible Asset Recognition
Purchase accounting would have required the identification and fair-value measurement of **acquired intangible assets** such as customer relationships, developed technology (Grok LLM), trade names, and the X platform's user base. Under common-control accounting, no new intangible assets were recognized. The existing intangible asset balances (including the X brand, which was impaired by $3,775 million in 2023 and reclassified as finite-lived with a 5-year useful life) reflect X's original purchase accounting from 2022, not any fresh measurement.

### 4. Purchase Price Allocation
No **purchase price allocation** was performed. Under purchase accounting, the acquirer must disclose a detailed allocation of consideration to each major class of assets acquired and liabilities assumed, including contingent liabilities. This allocation—which would have revealed the implied value of xAI's AI compute infrastructure, X's user base and advertising platform, and other key assets—is entirely absent.

### 5. Standalone Pre-Merger SpaceX Financials
Because the consolidated financials are retrospectively recast to combine SpaceX, xAI, and X **for all periods presented**, investors **cannot isolate standalone SpaceX-only historical results** (i.e., the Space and Connectivity segments operating without xAI/X). Every historical income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement in the filing reflects the combined entity. While segment-level disclosures partially mitigate this (Space, Connectivity, and AI segments are reported separately), the consolidated top-line figures, tax provisions, debt balances, and cash flows are fully commingled for all historical periods.

### 6. Standalone xAI and X Financial Statements
The filing explicitly states that **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**. Under purchase accounting, the acquired entity's standalone pre-acquisition financials would typically be filed (or incorporated by reference) as part of the registration statement. Here, because the entities are treated as always having been combined, no standalone xAI or X financial statements exist in the filing.

### 7. Historical Related-Party Transactions Between the Entities
All historical transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X that were previously classified as **related-party transactions** have been **retroactively eliminated in consolidation**. This means investors cannot observe:
- The scale, pricing, or terms of any inter-company service agreements, data-sharing arrangements, or cost-sharing agreements that existed before the formal mergers
- Whether any entity was subsidizing another (e.g., whether SpaceX was providing below-market launch services to xAI/X, or whether X was providing below-market compute or data access to xAI)
- The volume and nature of cash flows between the entities

### 8. Implied Acquisition Premium/Discount
Under purchase accounting, the relationship between the purchase consideration and the fair value of net assets acquired reveals the **acquisition premium** (or bargain purchase gain). Because no fair value measurement of net assets was performed, and because the consideration included both cash ($2.947 billion) and equity (443.4 million pre-split shares at an implied $75.46/share for cash-election purposes), investors cannot calculate the implied premium or discount SpaceX paid to acquire xAI.

### 9. xAI Preferred Stock on an As-Converted Historical Basis
As a specific exception within the recast, **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** is presented in its historical legal form for all periods prior to the February 2026 merger—it is **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted-to-SpaceX-equity basis. This means investors cannot observe what the consolidated capital structure would have looked like on an as-converted basis in prior periods; the conversion is only recognized prospectively in Q1 2026.

---

### Summary Table

| Information Category | Purchase Accounting | Common-Control Accounting (This Filing) |
|---|---|---|
| Asset/liability fair values at merger | Observable (step-up/step-down) | **Not observable** (historical carrying amounts) |
| Merger-generated goodwill | Recognized and disclosed | **Not recorded** |
| New intangible assets | Identified and measured at FV | **Not recorded** |
| Purchase price allocation | Detailed disclosure required | **Not performed** |
| Standalone acquirer (SpaceX) pre-merger financials | Preserved unchanged | **Not separable** (fully recast) |
| Standalone target (xAI/X) financials | Filed or incorporated | **Not provided** |
| Pre-merger related-party transactions | Visible until acquisition date | **Retroactively eliminated** |
| Acquisition premium/discount | Calculable | **Not calculable** |

---

**Sources:**
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026)"
    }
  ]
}

results.content:
## Historical Information No Longer Observable Under Common-Control Accounting vs. Purchase Accounting

The SpaceX S-1 retrospectively recasts all historical periods under **common-control accounting** (ASC 805-50) because Elon Musk held a controlling/majority voting interest in SpaceX, xAI, and X Holdings during all periods presented. This treatment fundamentally differs from purchase accounting (ASC 805 acquisition method) and renders the following categories of historical information **no longer observable**:

---

### 1. Fair Value of Acquired Net Assets
Under purchase accounting, the acquirer must remeasure all acquired assets and assumed liabilities to their **acquisition-date fair values** (the "step-up" or "step-down"). Under common-control accounting, the net assets of xAI and X were combined into SpaceX's consolidated financials at their **historical carrying amounts** (book values). Investors cannot observe what the fair value of xAI's or X's net assets—such as AI infrastructure, the Grok model, the X platform, user data, or spectrum rights—would have been at the merger dates.

### 2. Merger-Generated Goodwill
Purchase accounting would have recognized **goodwill** equal to the excess of the purchase consideration over the fair value of identifiable net assets. Given that SpaceX paid $2.947 billion in cash plus 321.7 million Class A shares and 121.7 million Class B shares (pre-split) for xAI, a substantial goodwill balance would likely have been created. The filing explicitly states: **"No new goodwill or other intangible assets have been recorded"** as a result of the mergers. The $11,681 million in goodwill on the consolidated balance sheet as of March 31, 2026 (of which $11,166 million is in the AI segment) reflects only **pre-existing goodwill** from X's original 2022 take-private acquisition—not any incremental goodwill from the SpaceX/xAI or xAI/X mergers.

### 3. New Intangible Asset Recognition
Purchase accounting would have required the identification and fair-value measurement of **acquired intangible assets** such as customer relationships, developed technology (Grok LLM), trade names, and the X platform's user base. Under common-control accounting, no new intangible assets were recognized. The existing intangible asset balances (including the X brand, which was impaired by $3,775 million in 2023 and reclassified as finite-lived with a 5-year useful life) reflect X's original purchase accounting from 2022, not any fresh measurement.

### 4. Purchase Price Allocation
No **purchase price allocation** was performed. Under purchase accounting, the acquirer must disclose a detailed allocation of consideration to each major class of assets acquired and liabilities assumed, including contingent liabilities. This allocation—which would have revealed the implied value of xAI's AI compute infrastructure, X's user base and advertising platform, and other key assets—is entirely absent.

### 5. Standalone Pre-Merger SpaceX Financials
Because the consolidated financials are retrospectively recast to combine SpaceX, xAI, and X **for all periods presented**, investors **cannot isolate standalone SpaceX-only historical results** (i.e., the Space and Connectivity segments operating without xAI/X). Every historical income statement, balance sheet, and cash flow statement in the filing reflects the combined entity. While segment-level disclosures partially mitigate this (Space, Connectivity, and AI segments are reported separately), the consolidated top-line figures, tax provisions, debt balances, and cash flows are fully commingled for all historical periods.

### 6. Standalone xAI and X Financial Statements
The filing explicitly states that **separate financial statements for xAI and X are not provided**. Under purchase accounting, the acquired entity's standalone pre-acquisition financials would typically be filed (or incorporated by reference) as part of the registration statement. Here, because the entities are treated as always having been combined, no standalone xAI or X financial statements exist in the filing.

### 7. Historical Related-Party Transactions Between the Entities
All historical transactions between SpaceX, xAI, and X that were previously classified as **related-party transactions** have been **retroactively eliminated in consolidation**. This means investors cannot observe:
- The scale, pricing, or terms of any inter-company service agreements, data-sharing arrangements, or cost-sharing agreements that existed before the formal mergers
- Whether any entity was subsidizing another (e.g., whether SpaceX was providing below-market launch services to xAI/X, or whether X was providing below-market compute or data access to xAI)
- The volume and nature of cash flows between the entities

### 8. Implied Acquisition Premium/Discount
Under purchase accounting, the relationship between the purchase consideration and the fair value of net assets acquired reveals the **acquisition premium** (or bargain purchase gain). Because no fair value measurement of net assets was performed, and because the consideration included both cash ($2.947 billion) and equity (443.4 million pre-split shares at an implied $75.46/share for cash-election purposes), investors cannot calculate the implied premium or discount SpaceX paid to acquire xAI.

### 9. xAI Preferred Stock on an As-Converted Historical Basis
As a specific exception within the recast, **xAI Redeemable Convertible Preferred Stock** is presented in its historical legal form for all periods prior to the February 2026 merger—it is **not** retrospectively recast on an as-converted-to-SpaceX-equity basis. This means investors cannot observe what the consolidated capital structure would have looked like on an as-converted basis in prior periods; the conversion is only recognized prospectively in Q1 2026.

---

### Summary Table

| Information Category | Purchase Accounting | Common-Control Accounting (This Filing) |
|---|---|---|
| Asset/liability fair values at merger | Observable (step-up/step-down) | **Not observable** (historical carrying amounts) |
| Merger-generated goodwill | Recognized and disclosed | **Not recorded** |
| New intangible assets | Identified and measured at FV | **Not recorded** |
| Purchase price allocation | Detailed disclosure required | **Not performed** |
| Standalone acquirer (SpaceX) pre-merger financials | Preserved unchanged | **Not separable** (fully recast) |
| Standalone target (xAI/X) financials | Filed or incorporated | **Not provided** |
| Pre-merger related-party transactions | Visible until acquisition date | **Retroactively eliminated** |
| Acquisition premium/discount | Calculable | **Not calculable** |

---
